svg.title: Taxonomy of spatial cognition tasks mapped to three axes: spatial type, reference frame, and environmental scale
svg.desc: A structured diagram showing how the seven benchmark tasks map to three axes from spatial cognition theory: Uttal 2x2 (intrinsic/extrinsic, static/dynamic), reference frame (egocentric/allocentric/switching), and Siegel and White environmental scale (landmark, route, survey).

g.text: Axis 1 — Uttal 2×2: what type of spatial ability?
Axis 3 — Environmental scale: what level of knowledge?
Your tasks — classified across the three axes
T1 — Spatial relation: agent ↔ visible objects
Axis 1: extrinsic–static · Axis 2: egocentric · Axis 3: landmark
Direction (left/right/above/below) and metric (near/far) from current agent viewpoint
T2 — Spatial relation: agent ↔ non-visible objects
Axis 1: extrinsic–static · Axis 2: egocentric + allocentric · Axis 3: route
Requires spatial memory across frames — UNIQUE to your benchmark
T3 — Spatial relation: two visible objects
Axis 1: extrinsic–static · Axis 2: egocentric · Axis 3: landmark
Direction and metric between object pairs from agent perspective
T4 — Spatial relation: N visible objects
Axis 1: extrinsic–static · Axis 2: egocentric · Axis 3: landmark → survey
Multi-object reasoning; complexity scales with N — hardest at large N
Axis 1: extrinsic–dynamic · Axis 2: frame-switching · Axis 3: route → survey
Most cognitively demanding — requires mental simulation of unobserved states
Axis 1: extrinsic–static · Axis 2: allocentric · Axis 3: landmark
RCC-8 relations (inside, overlap, touch, EC, TPP/NTPP) — formally grounded
